chapter: Chapter 10: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

questions.question: Explain the role of the cornea and crystalline lens in focusing light in the human eye.
questions.answer: The cornea provides most of the refraction as light enters the eye, while the crystalline lens fine-tunes the focus by changing shape to form a sharp image on the retina.

questions.question: What is meant by the power of accommodation? How do ciliary muscles help in this process?
questions.answer: Power of accommodation is the eye's ability to adjust focal length for clear vision of near and far objects. Ciliary muscles contract to make the lens thicker for near objects and relax to make it thinner for distant objects.

questions.question: State the near point and far point for a normal human eye.
questions.answer: For a normal eye, the near point is 25 cm (least distance of distinct vision) and the far point is at infinity.

questions.question: Describe how myopia affects vision and why it occurs.
questions.answer: In myopia, the eye forms images of distant objects in front of the retina due to excessive curvature of the cornea/lens or elongated eyeball, causing blurred distant vision while near vision remains clear.

questions.question: How is myopia corrected? Explain with the type of lens used.
questions.answer: Myopia is corrected using a concave (diverging) lens, which diverges incoming parallel rays so that the image forms on the retina instead of in front of it.

questions.question: Explain the cause of hypermetropia and its effect on vision.
questions.answer: Hypermetropia occurs when the eyeball is too short or the lens has insufficient curvature, causing images of nearby objects to form behind the retina, leading to blurred near vision while distant vision is clear.
answer_outline: Causes: short eyeball or weak lens

questions.question: How is hypermetropia corrected using lenses?
questions.answer: It is corrected with a convex (converging) lens, which converges rays from nearby objects earlier so the image forms on the retina.

questions.question: What is presbyopia? Why does it occur and how is it corrected?
questions.answer: Presbyopia is the gradual loss of accommodation with age due to weakening of ciliary muscles and reduced lens flexibility. It is corrected using bifocal lenses (upper part for distant, lower for near vision).

questions.question: Describe the dispersion of white light through a prism.
questions.answer: When white light passes through a prism, different colours bend by different amounts due to varying refractive indices, splitting into a spectrum of violet, indigo, blue, green, yellow, orange, and red (VIBGYOR).

questions.question: Explain why the sky appears blue during the day.
questions.answer: Blue light has shorter wavelength and is scattered more strongly by air molecules in all directions (Rayleigh scattering), so when we look up, scattered blue light reaches our eyes from the atmosphere.

questions.question: Why do stars appear to twinkle?
questions.answer: Twinkling occurs due to atmospheric refraction; light from stars passes through varying density layers, bending irregularly and changing apparent position, causing brightness fluctuations.

questions.question: Why does the Sun appear reddish at sunrise and sunset?
questions.answer: Sunlight travels a longer path through the atmosphere at these times, scattering shorter wavelengths (blue/violet) more, so mostly longer red/orange wavelengths reach the observer.

questions.question: Why are danger signals red in colour?
questions.answer: Red light has the longest wavelength and scatters the least in fog, smoke, or dust, allowing it to travel farther and remain visible from a distance.

questions.question: Explain the formation of a rainbow.
questions.answer: Rainbow forms by refraction, dispersion, and total internal reflection of sunlight in raindrops: light bends entering drop, disperses into colours, reflects internally, and refracts out, creating the spectrum arc.

questions.question: What is atmospheric refraction? Give one example besides twinkling.
questions.answer: Atmospheric refraction is bending of light due to varying refractive index in air layers. Example: the Sun appears higher than actual position near horizon due to refraction.

questions.question: Why do planets not twinkle while stars do?
questions.answer: Planets are closer and appear as extended sources (discs), so refraction effects average out across the surface with no noticeable twinkling, unlike point-like stars.

questions.question: What is the Tyndall effect? Give a daily life example.
questions.answer: Tyndall effect is scattering of light by larger colloidal particles, making the beam visible. Example: sunlight beam visible in a dusty room or car headlights in fog.

questions.question: How does the pupil regulate light entering the eye?
questions.answer: The iris adjusts pupil size: contracts in bright light to reduce entry (smaller pupil) and dilates in dim light to allow more light (larger pupil).

questions.question: Why does the Sun appear flattened or oval near the horizon?
questions.answer: Atmospheric refraction bends rays from the lower edge more than the upper, making vertical diameter appear smaller while horizontal remains unchanged, flattening the Sun.

questions.question: Explain why advanced sunrise and delayed sunset occur.
questions.answer: Atmospheric refraction bends sunlight from below the horizon towards the observer, making the Sun appear above the horizon earlier at sunrise and later at sunset than geometric position.

questions.question: What is the function of the retina in vision?
questions.answer: The retina acts as a light-sensitive screen with rods and cones that convert light into electrical signals, which the optic nerve sends to the brain to form the image.

questions.question: Why is cataract considered a defect of the eye? How is it treated?
questions.answer: Cataract makes the lens cloudy/milky, reducing light transmission and causing blurred vision. It is treated by surgical replacement of the cloudy lens with an artificial one.

questions.question: Describe the role of the optic nerve.
questions.answer: The optic nerve carries electrical impulses generated by the retina to the brain, where they are interpreted as visual images.

questions.question: Why do we see a spectrum after dispersion but not in white light directly?
questions.answer: White light is a mixture of colours; dispersion separates them because each colour has a different speed and refractive index in the medium, bending differently.

questions.question: How does scattering explain why the colour of the clear sky is blue but appears dark above the atmosphere?
questions.answer: On Earth, blue scatters in all directions, making the sky blue. Above the atmosphere (no scattering medium), no light scatters towards the observer, so space appears dark.
